lessons.title: print and variables
lessons.body: In Python, `print()` shows something on the screen and variables store values. A variable is created the moment you assign a value to it with `=`. Try changing the name and running the example.
lessons.example:
# print() shows text on the screen
print('Hello, world!')

# A variable stores a value to use later
name = 'Ana'
age = 21

# f-strings insert variables inside the text
print(f'{name} is {age} years old')

lessons.title: Numbers and operations
lessons.body: Python has integers (`int`) and decimals (`float`). The operators are `+`, `-`, `*`, `/` (division always gives a float), `//` (integer division), `%` (remainder) and `**` (power).
lessons.example:
# Basic operations
a = 7
b = 2

print(a + b)   # 9
print(a / b)   # 3.5  (division always returns a float)
print(a // b)  # 3    (integer division drops the remainder)
print(a % b)   # 1    (remainder of the division)
print(a ** b)  # 49   (7 to the power of 2)

lessons.title: Strings
lessons.body: Strings are text between quotes. You can join them with `+`, repeat them with `*`, measure them with `len()` and transform them with methods like `.upper()` and `.lower()`.
lessons.example:
phrase = 'python is fun'

# len() counts the characters
print(len(phrase))

# Methods transform the string (without changing the original)
print(phrase.upper())
print(phrase.capitalize())

# Slices take pieces: [start:end]
print(phrase[0:6])

exercises.title: Your first greeting
exercises.prompt: Create a variable called `message` with the text 'Hello, Python!' and print it on the screen with print().
exercises.starterCode: # Create the variable and use print()

tests.name: variable message exists with the right text
tests.code: assert message == 'Hello, Python!', f"expected 'Hello, Python!', got {message!r}"

hints: Text in Python goes between single or double quotes.

exercises.title: Sum function
exercises.prompt: Write a function `add(a, b)` that returns the sum of the two numbers.
hints: Use the return keyword to give back the result.

exercises.title: Double the number
exercises.prompt: Write a function `double(n)` that returns twice n.
hints: Multiply n by 2 and return the result.

exercises.title: Shouting the text
exercises.prompt: Write a function `shout(text)` that returns the text in UPPERCASE followed by '!'. E.g.: shout('hi') → 'HI!'
hints: The .upper() method makes the string uppercase.
Join strings with +.

exercises.title: Rectangle area
exercises.prompt: Write a function `area(base, height)` that returns the area of a rectangle.
hints: The area is base times height (base * height).

exercises.title: Average of two numbers
exercises.prompt: Write a function `average(a, b)` that returns the arithmetic mean of two numbers.
hints: Add the two numbers and divide by 2.

exercises.title: Seconds to minutes
exercises.prompt: Write a function `minutes(seconds)` that returns how many whole minutes fit in the given number of seconds. E.g.: minutes(130) → 2
hints: Integer division // drops the remainder: 130 // 60 == 2.

exercises.title: Name initial
exercises.prompt: Write a function `initial(name)` that returns the first letter of the name in uppercase. E.g.: initial('ana') → 'A'
hints: name[0] takes the first letter.
Use .upper() to make it uppercase.

exercises.title: Discounted price
exercises.prompt: Write a function `discounted(price, percent)` that returns the price after applying the percentage discount. E.g.: discounted(100, 20) → 80.0
hints: Discount amount = price * percent / 100.
Subtract the discount from the price.

exercises.title: Text length
exercises.prompt: Write a function `length(text)` that returns how many characters the text has.
hints: The len() function counts the characters of a string.

exercises.title: Full name
exercises.prompt: Write a function `full_name(first, last)` that returns both names joined, separated by a space. E.g.: full_name('Ana', 'Silva') → 'Ana Silva'
hints: Use an f-string: f'{first} {last}'.
Or concatenate with +: first + ' ' + last.

exercises.title: Square of a number
exercises.prompt: Write a function `square(n)` that returns n squared.
hints: The ** operator raises to a power: n ** 2.

exercises.title: Change
exercises.prompt: Write a function `change(paid, price)` that returns how much change should be given back.
hints: The change is the amount paid minus the price (paid - price).

exercises.title: Average of three numbers
exercises.prompt: Write a function `average3(a, b, c)` that returns the arithmetic mean of the three numbers.
hints: Add the three numbers and divide by 3.

exercises.title: Reversed text
exercises.prompt: Write a function `reverse(text)` that returns the text backwards. E.g.: reverse('python') → 'nohtyp'
hints: Slicing with a negative step reverses the string: text[::-1].

exercises.title: Repeat text
exercises.prompt: Write a function `repeat(text, times)` that returns the text repeated the given number of times. E.g.: repeat('ab', 3) → 'ababab'
hints: The * operator repeats strings: text * times.

exercises.title: Celsius to Fahrenheit
exercises.prompt: Write a function `celsius_to_fahrenheit(c)` that converts a temperature in Celsius to Fahrenheit. Formula: F = C * 9/5 + 32
hints: Apply the formula directly: c * 9 / 5 + 32.

exercises.title: Spaces to hyphens
exercises.prompt: Write a function `text_with_hyphens(text)` that returns the text with every space replaced by a hyphen. E.g.: text_with_hyphens('a b c') → 'a-b-c'

tests.name: text_with_hyphens('a b c') == 'a-b-c'
tests.code: assert text_with_hyphens('a b c') == 'a-b-c', f"got {text_with_hyphens('a b c')!r}"

hints: The .replace(' ', '-') method swaps each space for a hyphen.
